text: The patient is a forty-two year old woman with two months history of progressive left upper abdominal crampy pain. Seedy abdomen revealed a concentric narrowing of distal transverse colon.
This was confirmed on colonoscopy to be a stenotic ulcerative cancer. Her CEA was normal.
The plan was to perform distal transverse colectomy, including the upper half of the descending colon, using the da Vinci robotic system.
The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg, with the da Vinci robotic system and surgical ports visibly arranged for the procedure.
The surgeon begins the procedure by exploring the abdominal cavity and localizing the lesion in the distal transverse colon using the da Vinci robotic system.
The surgeon visually examines the distal transverse colon, confirming the location of the lesion marked by an ink stain, with no evidence of carcinomatosis observed.
The surgeon lifts the transverse colon to expose the ligament of Treitz, then makes an incision on the mesocolon above the ligament using surgical instruments.
The surgeon performs dissection cranially above the pancreas and along the lateral side of the inferior mesenteric vein, using the da Vinci robotic system.
The surgeon encounters the vascular branches supplying the left colon, carefully dissecting, clipping, and dividing them to ensure proper isolation and control of blood flow.
The surgeon exposes the tissue plane anterior to the Toldt fascia, which serves as a protective layer for the underlying ureter and ovarian vessels.
The surgeon continues dissection along the cranial and lateral plane, revealing the pancreatic tail adjacent to the dissected area.
The surgeon carries the dissection up into the apex, reaching the parietal peritoneum of the splenocolic ligament. Upon incising the ligament, the spleen becomes visible.
The surgeon focuses on the transverse mesocolon, dissecting above the pancreas to access the lesser sac cavity, where the stomach is visible.
The surgeon continues dissection medially and laterally along the pancreas, detaching the transverse mesocolon from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon divides the transverse mesocolon toward the proximal line of resection, located approximately five to six centimeters from the lesion. The arterial branch from the middle colic artery is visibly divided during this process.
The surgeon clears the fat and omentum surrounding the transverse colon, followed by transection of the transverse colon using endo-GI staplers.
The surgeon focuses on the distal colon, dissecting the upper third of the descending colon until it is circumferentially free. The colon is then transected using endo-GI staplers.
The surgeon detaches the specimen from the renocolic and splenocolic ligaments, then returns to the lesser sac to divide the gastrocolic ligament, leaving most of it attached to the specimen.
The surgeon divides the remaining splenocolic ligament, completing the resection of the colon.
The surgical specimen is placed into a specimen bag, which remains in the surgical field as the team prepares to perform the reconstruction.
The surgeon mobilizes the proximal transverse colon, hepatic flexure, and ascending colon, ensuring there is no tension before initiating the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs an intracorporeal hand-sewn anastomosis on the enterocyte, using a running 3-0 silk suture to secure the posterior seromuscular layer.
The surgeon completes the remaining layers of the anastomosis using 3-0 V-lock sutures, which are visible as braided absorbable sutures that lock onto the tissue upon passage.
The completed anastomosis is visible, showing the connection between the mobilized sections of the colon with sutures securing the tissue.
The patient did well postoperatively. She passed flatus on the third postoperative day and was discharged on the following day.
